content.full_text:
April 8, 1981

A-81-04-018

Henry L. Smith, Jr.
The El Paso Company
1127 Allen Parkway
Houston, Texas 77001

Dear Mr. Smith:

This is in response to your letter dated March 9, 1981, in which
you proposed a method for employees of the El Paso Company to
contribute to PacPac-California, a recipient committee, by
authorizing payroll deductions. You asked whether the proposed
method would allow for compliance with reporting requirements
for recipient committees under the Political Reform Act of
1974.

I see no anticipate problems with the proposed method of deduction
or record keeping for contributions from employees of the
El Paso Company.

If you have any further need of assistance please feel free to
contact me.

Yours very truly,


Lynn Montgomery
Political Reform Consultant

LM/bb

THE
El Paso COMPANY

2727 ALLEN PARKWAY
P O BOX 2185
HOUSTON, TEXAS 77001

PHONE: 713-525-9400

March 9, 1981


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Gentlemen:

I am writing to request your opinion of a proposed course of
action by PASPAC-California which is a political action committee sponsored
by The El Paso Company and subsidiary and affiliated companies. The
Committee has reported to your jurisdiction. According to its bylaws,
it is involved only in nonfederal California elections. Our company also
sponsors other committees, each of which is involved in different kinds
of elections in other jurisdictions.

Many of our employees contribute to our committees by the
payroll deduction method based on written deduction requests signed by
the employees. Each deduction request specifies the total amount which
each employee wishes to contribute to all of our political action
committees combined, and the deduction request also indicates the
percentage of the total which the employee wishes to go to each of our
committees, including PASPAC-California. For many reasons, it is desired
to achieve flexibility concerning the percentage of each employee's
total contribution which goes to each of our committees, including
PASPAC-California. In order to avoid the necessity for repeated execution
of new payroll deduction requests, we propose to suggest to our employees
who use the payroll deduction system that they execute a written document
appointing a specific person in our company as their agent for the
apportionment of their total contributions among our various committees.
Under this optional agency document, the agent will be authorized
by the employee to change the apportionment of his total contribution
from time to time according to the present needs of our various committees.
Each contributor will be advised, via our Committee newsletter, when the
apportionment is about to be changed by the agent. The contributor will
also be informed of the new percentages which will determine how much of

Fair Political Practices Commission
March 9, 1981
Page Two


his or her total payroll deduction will go to each of our various committees,
including PASPAC-California. At the end of every year, the contributor
will be given a statement detailing how much he or she contributed to
our various committees during the year. At any time during the year the
employee can determine how much he or she has contributed to each of our
committees by simply contacting the treasurer of our committees. The
document authorizing the agent to apportion contributions can be revoked
at any time.

This new device of an agent for apportionment of contributions
would be offered to our employees who use the payroll deduction system
for their contributions. It will be entirely optional. Employees could
still designate fixed percentages of their total contribution to go to
each of our committees. Or they may elect not to use the payroll deduc-
tion system at all; instead they may contribute by separate checks from
time to time as they see fit. Of course, an employee may choose not to
contribute at all to our committees. The agency device that we propose
to offer would simply eliminate the necessity of suggesting to our
employees that they execute, repeatedly, new payroll deduction requests
in response to the changing relative financial needs of our several
committees.

We would appreciate your opinion stating whether this proposal
would create any problems under your state election law. We have
researched the law, and we cannot find any problems with our proposal.
We would appreciate your opinion as soon as possible. Thank you very
much.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Henry L. Smith, Jr.
Attorney

dsh

sections.question: You asked whether the proposed method would allow El Paso compliance with reporting requirements for recipient committees under the Political Reform Act of 1974.
sections.conclusion: I see no anticipated problems with the proposed method of deduction or record keeping for contributions from employees of an El Paso Company.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a proposed payroll deduction method with an agent authorized to apportion employee contributions among multiple political action committees would comply with Political Reform Act reporting requirements for recipient committees.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC found no compliance problems with the proposed payroll deduction and apportionment system, including the use of an agent to allocate contributions among multiple political action committees.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request (dated March 9, 1981) and FPPC response (dated April 5, 1981). Some OCR quality issues but core Q/C sections identifiable. Year discrepancy noted (document header says 1951 but content clearly from 1981). Response letter is brief without detailed analysis.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You asked whether the proposed method would allow El Paso compliance with reporting requirements for recipient committees under the Political Reform Act of 1974.

CONCLUSION: I see no anticipated problems with the proposed method of deduction or record keeping for contributions from employees of an El Paso Company.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advised that El Paso Company's proposed method allowing employees to authorize a payroll deduction with an agent managing the apportionment of contributions among multiple political action committees presents no anticipated problems under the Political Reform Act of 1974.
